Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). But "illnesses change greatly and are without constancy, whereas recipes are fixed and limited" (病多變而無常 方一定而有限). And how could one
“respond to inconstant illnesses with limited recipes”(以有限之方應無常之病)? $ ^{10} $ Wang
received from Dai himself the cases by Zhu that Wang Xing was prefacing. $ ^{11} $

Wang Xing attributed the “extraordinary efficacy” (奇驗) of Dai Sigong’s cures to his adoption of Danxi’s flexible methods. page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: Most elite physicians from the twelfth century onward, while recognizing that there was “constancy” (chang 常) to illnesses, emphasized that the best healers should also react to “transformations” (bian 變), the events that diverged from the regular ways in which reality was supposed to unfold. “Constancy” itself referred not to unchanging essences but to the regular and predictable way in which processes developed. The emergence of case files as a distinct genre partly stemmed from this flexible conception of illness. First, despite many claims to the contrary, Wang Ji's work was not the first autonomous work to contain the cases of a single physician. $ ^{4} $ Zhang Congzheng's Ten Configurations and Three Therapies (Shixing sanliao 十形三療), an anthology of Zhang's cases, was edited soon after his death (1228) by his disciples, and was circulating on its own by the fourteenth cen ...

As Dai explained in a text written in 1358 or 1359: For the illnesses [Zhu] treated, what the illness looked like, what excellent remedy he applied, and the drinking of what drugs led to healing [were recorded]. From the past until now, for the efficacious [cases]: who [his patient] was, from what village in what county [he or she came], the names of the interested party, and what [Zhu] had witnessed and heard are clearly documented. $ ^{7} $ 其所治病凡機病之狀何如 施何良方 飲何藥而愈 自前至今 驗者何人 何縣里 主名 得諸見聞 班班可紀 Song Lian even claimed that Zhu's "disciples possess books [of these records], classified by illnesses" (門人類證有書). $ ^{8} $ It is no coincidence that Zhu Zhenheng's disciples and admirers were the leading transmitters of this flexible art of prescription during the early Ming dynasty. Zhu's own case files played a role. Wang Xing's 王行 preface (dated 1377, less than 20 years after Zhu's death) to a compendium of Zhu's cases explained how unusual Zhu's approach had been. $ ^{9} $ Most physicians, Wang claimed, instructed "to treat such-and-such an illness with this remedy, and that such-and-such a remedy treats such-and-such an illness" (治某病以某方以某方治某病而已). Both in his approach to madness and in his general doctrinal discussions, Wang often tackled issues that were discussed in the contemporary medical literature.
